identity.project_summary: Collaborative project with Virtanen Lab based on a single-center, prospective cohort trial (ISRCTN91953024) of 258 women under 31 years old with CIN2 managed with active surveillance and followed for up to 24 months. The project aims to identify spatial TME and transcriptomic differences between progressive, regressive, or persistent lesions. 67 biopsies from 60 patients were selected (15 quick regressors in 1 year, 15 slow regressors in 2 years, 30 non-regressors, plus 3 matched invasive carcinoma progressions). Integrates multi-pass t-CycIF proteomics (Ella/Naipunya/Saundarya) and GeoMx spatial transcriptomics. Implements cell segmentation via Oskari Lehtonen's pipeline, Tribus cell calling, spatial Recurrent Cellular Neighborhoods (RCN) mapping (DBSCAN/spatial count at 100um), spatial LDA, and correlates cell type proportions with clinical outcomes, HPV high-risk/low-risk types (including HPV16 status), and E4 viral replication relocation status.
identity.responsible: Ella Anttila (Anni Virtanen, Oskari Lehtonen, Saundarya Shah, Zhihan Liang, Kaiyang Zhang, Anniina Färkkilä, Naipunya Guruprasad)

## project overview

*Cin2 project is a collaborative project with Virtanen Lab. The project
is based on* a follow-up study conducted at the University of Helsinki,
where patients diagnosed with CIN2 are monitored for two years or more.
Our aim is to find out whether there are any differences in the
microenvironment or in spatial transcriptomics between the progressive,
the regressive or the persistent lesions.

Speaker notes: RCN5 (Stromal core) dominates at 1.1M cells — the connective tissue background
RCN3 (Dysplastic core) at 70% dysplastic epithelial is the cleanest tumour niche
RCN4 (Normal epithelial) at 62% normal is well-separated from RCN3
RCN1 (B cell-rich) is the most distinctive immune niche — 43% B cells plus 12% CD8+, very unusual composition, but small (47k cells)
RCN7 (Immune-stromal-dysplastic interface) is the most mixed — this is the invasion front/tumour edge
RCN8 (Myeloid-T cell inflamed) has 15% M2, 15% CD8+, 12% CD4+ — a classically inflamed niche with high M2 content suggesting possible immune suppression
RCN6 (Stromal core 2) only 82% self-adjacent vs RCN5's 95% — clearly a transitional zone, not a pure stromal niche. The 12% spillover to RCN5 confirms it

Speaker notes: But after Centered Log-Ratio (CLR) transformation, dysplastic rcn vanishes in count but there is another rcn thats enrtiched but in LDA we still see rcn enriched in dysplatic cells survivedSo if a patient has 40% Stromal core, the remaining 60% must split among the other 7 RCNs. This means RCN proportions are not independent — when one goes up, others must go down. This creates fake correlations between RCNs that can either hide real differences or create false ones. CLR (Centered Log-Ratio) breaks this constraint by log-transforming and centering each RCN relative to the geometric mean of all RCNs for that patient. After CLR, the values are no longer tied to summing to 1, so each RCN can be tested independently.

Speaker notes: Every year, hundreds of thousands of women are diagnosed with the various pre –cancerous stages of the cervix. 

It doesn't appear suddenly; it develops through a stepwise process, starting from an HPV infection, progressing through precancerous lesions, and eventually into invasive cancer.

most Human Papilloma Virus infections are actually cleared by our immune system within one to two years. But HPV specifically targets the basal layer of the epithelium at the squamocolumnar junction, which is. a region in the cervix where two different cell types meet. This transition zone is particularly vulnerable because the cells there are actively remodelling, which makes it easier for the virus to infect and persist in.

Speaker notes: When the virus persists, it can lead to abnormal changes in the cervical cells called dysplasia. 
These changes are graded from CIN1 to CIN3 based on severity and CIN2 sits right in the middle, because it captures a mixture of CIN1, which are HPV-producing and relatively benign, and CIN3-like cells, which are more transformed and at higher risk of progression. ## Slide 4
BACKGROUND
WHY & WHICH OF THESE LESIONS IN WOMEN REGRESS SPONTANEOUSLY WHILE OTHERS PROGRESS?
Speaker notes: The current standard treatment for cervical pre-cancer is LEEP also known as Loop Electrosurgical Excision Procedure . In this procedure, a thin wire loop is used to remove the abnormal tissues from the cervix. It sounds straightforward, but it comes with real consequences like bleeding, infection, and importantly, complications in future pregnancies such as preterm birth.

What makes this even more problematic is that in a 2 year surveillance trial done right here at HUS, revealed that in 50 to 60 percent of CIN2 lesions in woomen actually regress on their own, without any treatment. That means the majority of women who undergo this procedure they may have never needed in the first place.

And that forms the core question driving this project: why and which CIN2 lesions regress spontaneously, while others progress — so that we can move towards a more personalised management and avoid unnecessary overtreatment.

WHAT IS CIN2?
Speaker notes: To understand CIN2 a bit better — CIN, or Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia, is divided into grades 1, 2, and 3. CIN2 represents moderate dysplasia.
This means that the cells in the cervical epithelium start to look abnormal, but they haven’t yet invaded deeper tissues.
What makes CIN2 especially interesting is that it’s quite mixed biologically — it often contains both CIN1-like areas, which are still producing HPV, and CIN3-like areas, which are transformed and no longer produce the virus.
Because of this mix, the underlying biology of CIN2 isn’t fully understood. Some lesions actually regress on their own, while others progress toward CIN3 and, in some cases, cervical cancer.
And that uncertainty is one of the key reasons behind our project — to better understand the molecular and spatial features that distinguish CIN2 lesions that regress from those that progress.

efly go over the current management of CIN2 lesions.
The standard treatment is the LEEP procedure, or Loop Electrosurgical Excision Procedure, which removes abnormal cervical tissue using a low-voltage electrical current — as shown here on the left.
Although LEEP is effective, it can be associated with complications, such as bleeding, infection, and even pregnancy-related complications later on.
What makes CIN2 particularly challenging is that around 50 to 60 percent of these lesions regress spontaneously, especially in younger women under 30. Because of this, active surveillance — meaning careful follow-up without immediate surgery — can be a reasonable management option for selected patients.
This clinical uncertainty — whether to treat or to observe — highlights the need for better biomarkers and molecular understanding of CIN2, which is exactly what our project aims to address.”

Speaker notes: Based on the uncertainty around CIN2 — where some lesions regress and others progress — the main aim of this project is to uncover the mechanisms of regression in CIN2.
To do this, we’re combining three complementary approaches:
Histopathology, to study the tissue morphology and cellular context;
Spatial proteomics, to analyze protein expression and localization in the tissue microenvironment; and
Transcriptomics, to explore gene expression patterns that might distinguish regressing from persistent lesions.

By integrating these layers of data, we hope to gain a more comprehensive understanding of what drives regression — and ultimately identify potential biomarkers to guide clinical decision-making.

Speaker notes: Our study is based on a clinical prospective trial that followed 258 women under the age of 31 diagnosed with CIN2.
Instead of undergoing immediate treatment, these women were managed with active surveillance, meaning that they were monitored carefully without surgical removal of the lesion.
The follow-up period was 24 months, during which participants underwent colposcopy, cytology, and biopsies every six months to evaluate how their lesions changed over time.
As shown in the flowchart, we observed different outcomes — around 64% of the lesions regressed, about 14% showed partial regression, and around 16% progressed to more severe disease.

Speaker notes: Here’s an overview of the project workflow, showing how we process and analyze the CIN2 tissue samples.
All our analyses start from the same set of tissue slides, which are then used for three complementary approaches.
First, We developed an AI-based model to identify features associated with regression from Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) images, extracting features such as neoplastic nuclear morphology and immune cell clustering.
Then, we apply t-CyCIF, which allows us to visualize and quantify dozens of protein markers in the same tissue section at single-cell resolution. 
Finally, we use GeoMX spatial transcriptomics to measure gene expression in selected regions of interest, identified based on the H&E and t-CyCIF data.
Together, these methods give us a multi-layered view of each lesion — combining morphology, protein expression, and gene expression — which will help us understand the mechanisms of regression in CIN2.”

Speaker notes: Here are the top ten features identified by Oskari´s model as predictive of CIN2 regression within two years.
Several interesting patterns emerged. First, smaller colposcopic lesion size and lower intensity of neoplastic nuclei were both associated with regression — suggesting that less severe morphological changes may predict a favorable outcome.
Second, features related to the immune microenvironment appeared repeatedly. For example, a higher proportion of immune cells within the lesion and a greater number of immune cell clusters were predictive of regression, indicating that a more active immune response could support lesion clearance.

Speaker notes: Next, I’ll go through our t-CyCIF workflow, which we use to characterize the cellular and immune composition of the CIN2 lesions in more detail.
We analyzed a total of 65 samples, divided into different outcome groups — including non-regressors, slow- and quick-regressors, and a few carcinoma and progressor samples for comparison.
Using t-CyCIF, we can stain the same tissue section for dozens of markers through repeated rounds of labeling and imaging.
The panel includes markers for different cell lineages — such as epithelial, immune, and stromal cells — as well as functional markers, including proliferation (Ki-67), immune checkpoints (PD-1, PD-L1), HPV-related proteins (E4), and antigen presentation (HLA).
The images are processed through a pipeline involving illumination correction, stitching with ASHLAR, segmentation, and quantification and CyLinter for quality control and data analysis.

This gives us single-cell–level data describing both the tissue architecture and the functional state of the cells, allowing us to compare regressing and persistent lesions in unprecedented detail.

Speaker notes: Finally, this slide shows our GeoMX spatial transcriptomics workflow, which we use to study gene expression in selected tissue regions.
We selected the samples based on the H&E results — choosing regressor samples that showed the strongest predictive features of regression, and progressor samples that showed the least of these features. We also included all lesions that progressed into invasive cancer, as well as carcinoma samples for comparison.
Using H&E images together with t-CyCIF guidance, we selected specific regions of interest for profiling on the GeoMX platform, allowing us to measure gene expression while preserving spatial context.
The ROIs were chosen to capture key tissue compartments — including the lesion–stroma interface, normal epithelium, lesion areas, immune cell clusters, and normal stroma.
In total, we analyzed 243 regions of interest and 374 areas of illumination, covering both lesion and stromal compartments.
This approach allows us to link gene expression patterns with the morphological and proteomic features from the H&E and t-CyCIF data, providing a more complete picture of CIN2 biology.”

Speaker notes: our main goal in this project is to understand why some HPV infections regress to normal, while others progress.
From previous H&E work that ella mentioned, it was discovered that certain cell types and their spatial organization contribute to whether a lesion regresses or progresses.To explore this further, we first perform cell phenotyping to identify the major cell populations, then use meta-clustering to define more specific cell types, and finally carry out spatial analysis to see how these cells are arranged and interact within the tissue.

By combining both, we can not only identify the cell types, but also their cell states within the microenvironment —This is particularly important for spotting proliferating epithelial cells, that could be pre cancerous cells.

Speaker notes: Looking at the preliminary results from the 45 samples, this plot shows the proportion of different cell types identified by Tribus across samples.
From the literature, we expected to see a strong presence of stromal cells and that’s exactly what we observe here in grey.
We also see clear variation in the proportions of epithelial, lymphoid, and myeloid cells, which is our main focus in these CIN2 lesions. This variation already hints at diversity within the cohort.
The dysplastic epithelial cells, shown in purple, are particularly interesting since these are associated with the precancerous regions.
These are still preliminary observations, but they give us a good foundation to start exploring how cell compositions might differ between regressor and non-regressor groups.
